mappings.rationaleAI: Galen's work as the dominant medical authority covers the acceptance of Galen in the Greco-Roman world (A.1.d.iii) and the preservation of his medical heritage in medieval monasteries and Arabian medicine (A.1.e.i).
Immunisation from Jenner's vaccination through modern vaccine development covers improvement in vaccination techniques (A.1.h.iii) and advances in immunology (A.3.b).

The history of human struggle against disease from miasma theory through germ theory, epidemiology, and antibiotics spans the full history of medicine from ancient through modern times (A.1, A.2.c, A.3).
Harvey's 1628 discovery of blood circulation addresses discoveries concerning the circulation of blood (A.1.g.i), while Galen's earlier theories connect to Greco-Roman medicine (A.1.d.iii).
The history of drugs covers improvement in treatment of disease (A.1.h.iii), the discovery of anesthesia (A.2.d), and advances in chemotherapy including antibiotics (A.3.a).
The four humours from Hippocrates through Galen directly covers the charter of medical conduct and theories on disease (A.1.d.ii) and the acceptance of Galen as authority (A.1.d.iii).

Vitalism's iatrochemical and iatrophysical theories relate to 17th-century debates about life as chemical processes versus physical mechanisms (A.1.g.ii), while the cell theory connects to new doctrines in the 19th century (A.2.a).
Pasteur's germ theory, disproof of spontaneous generation, and development of vaccines for rabies and anthrax directly cover establishment of bacteriology (A.2.c) and advances in immunology (A.3.b).
The history of anaesthetics from early methods through ether and chloroform directly covers the discovery and use of anesthesia (A.2.d) and the importance of anesthesia to modern surgery (A.4.a).
Blood covers Harvey's circulation discovery (A.1.g.i), Galen's earlier theories (A.1.d.iii), and blood transfusion techniques (A.4.d).
The history of anatomical knowledge covers Galen's authority (A.1.d.iii) and Renaissance improvements in anatomical theory through Vesalius (A.1.f.i).
Microbiology from van Leeuwenhoek through Pasteur and Koch covers the establishment of bacteriology and germ theory (A.2.c) and antibiotics (A.3.a).
Penicillin's discovery and development as the first widely used antibiotic directly covers advances in chemotherapy and antibiotics (A.3.a).
The origins of infectious disease cover the germ theory revolution (A.2.c) and the relationship between human ecology and pandemic spread connecting to tropical medicine (A.3.d.iii).

Bacteriophages cover contributions to molecular biology through the Hershey-Chase experiment and their renewed importance given antibiotic resistance, connecting to bacteriology (A.2.c) and chemotherapy developments (A.3.a).
Al-Kindi's contributions to medicine connect to the role of Arabian medicine in preserving and advancing the medical heritage (A.1.e.i).
Alchemy's connection to Paracelsus relates to the work of Paracelsus in Renaissance medicine (A.1.f.iii).
Genetics from Mendel through the Human Genome Project connects to new doctrines and laws including the Mendelian laws in 19th-century medicine (A.2.a).
